content.text: in Claude apps, the most important fact is that Claude’s API is

: your application must manage history and send the relevant conversation back on each request, because the model does not automatically remember prior API calls for you. In practice, that means the best production pattern is usually

: keep short-lived interactive state in the client for responsiveness, but keep the canonical conversation history on the server or in durable storage for consistency, recovery, auth, and long-term continuity.

The Claude Messages API does not maintain server-side conversation memory for your app session. Multiple sources in the search results state that every request must include the relevant prior messages, including assistant turns and tool interactions, if you want Claude to answer in context. So the real architectural question is not “does Claude store history?” but “where should
A useful mental model is to separate
. The AI SDK documentation defines AI state as the serializable state shared with the model, and for chat apps that usually means the conversation history plus metadata like

or timestamps, while UI state includes transient presentation concerns such as scroll position, expanded tool panels, or selected artifacts. This separation is valuable because only some state should travel back to Claude, and only some state needs durable persistence.
Client-owned history means the browser holds the message list and sends it to your API route on each turn. The AI SDK’s default
example works this way: the hook manages
on the client and posts them to
, where the server converts them to model messages and streams a response back. This pattern is simple, fast to build, and excellent for prototypes or lightweight chat experiences.

Client-side ownership gives you immediate optimistic updates, low-latency local editing, and a very direct mental model for UI code. It is especially convenient when the conversation is ephemeral, anonymous, or intentionally not persisted, such as a temporary helper widget or a demo chat. It also works naturally with

The main problem with client-only state is that it is fragile and not authoritative. A refresh, tab close, auth change, or multi-device scenario can lose or desynchronize the conversation unless you also persist it somewhere durable. It also increases the risk of sending stale, tampered, or incomplete histories back to the server unless you validate everything carefully.

Server-owned history means the backend stores the canonical chat transcript, usually in a database, cache, workflow state, or session store, and reconstructs the history for each Claude call. In this pattern, the client often sends only the latest user message plus a chat ID, and the server loads the prior turns, appends the new one, calls Claude, and persists the assistant response when complete. This is the strongest production default for authenticated apps and serious multi-turn workflows.

Server ownership gives you durable persistence, better security, easier auditing, shared access across devices, and stronger control over what actually reaches Claude. It also makes it easier to trim, summarize, redact, or reorder history centrally before each model call. In enterprise or regulated environments, this central control is often more important than the simplicity of purely client-side state.

The trade-off is complexity and potentially more round-trips. You must manage persistence, IDs, concurrency, regeneration behavior, and session authorization, and you often need a client cache or local mirror anyway for smooth UX during streaming. If overdone, server-owned history can make a simple chat app feel heavier than necessary.
apps, the best pattern is hybrid: the
client holds the active working copy
for rendering and interactivity, while the
server owns the durable source of truth

. In other words, let the browser manage the current visible transcript during a live session, but persist and validate the canonical history server-side. This gives you good UX without giving up consistency or recoverability.

Hybrid architecture matches how chat products actually behave. Users expect immediate local responsiveness, but they also expect reload survival, mobile continuity, and server-trusted history when they come back later. Separating “what the user sees right now” from “what the backend trusts long term” is usually the cleanest way to satisfy both needs.
A good ownership split often looks like this:
: current input, current streamed assistant message, local optimistic messages, UI-only flags, expanded/collapsed tool sections, scroll behavior.
: canonical transcript, message IDs, tool results, attachments metadata, summaries, usage stats, auth ownership, retention policy.
: only the model-relevant subset of server-approved history, possibly summarized or trimmed.
This avoids polluting model input with UI noise and avoids trusting the browser as the final authority.
The AI SDK docs explicitly show both styles. The default example posts the full
array from the client, but the transport customization examples show

sending only the latest message and a chat ID, with the server loading prior messages itself. That second pattern is the better fit when the server owns the conversation history and the client should not be responsible for resending the entire thread.
A simplified server-owned transport pattern is:
Then the server route loads the stored transcript, appends the new message, and calls Claude.
Why this matters for Claude specifically

Because Claude is stateless at the API layer, serialization bugs or dropped turns directly damage answer quality. If your client accidentally omits a tool result, assistant turn, or prior user instruction, Claude will behave as if that information never existed. Centralizing canonical history on the server reduces the chance that the browser sends an incomplete or corrupted transcript during complicated sessions.
Tool use makes server ownership stronger

Once your app uses Claude tools, server ownership becomes even more attractive. Tool interactions are part of the effective conversation history, and several sources note that all tool-related exchanges must be preserved if you want consistent multi-turn behavior. Since tool execution often already happens server-side, keeping the authoritative transcript there as well is usually cleaner than reconstructing it from the browser.
to persist history. Common patterns include persisting on

, persisting incrementally during the stream, or persisting the user turn immediately and the assistant turn when the stream completes. In most apps, a strong compromise is: save the user message as soon as it is accepted, stream the assistant response to the client, and commit the final assistant message after completion or after safe checkpoints.

The Upstash article in the results recommends server-side generated message IDs for persistence consistency across sessions and to prevent conflicts. This is a very good production rule because regenerated answers, resumed sessions, and multi-device sync are easier when IDs come from the backend rather than temporary browser state. Client-generated IDs can still be useful as provisional optimistic IDs, but the canonical IDs should usually be assigned server-side.
Conversation history ownership affects regenerate behavior too. The AI SDK docs show a trigger-based pattern where the server receives

, finds the target message in stored history, slices the conversation appropriately, and generates a replacement response from that point. This is much easier to do correctly when the server owns the canonical transcript rather than relying on the browser to reconstruct or mutate the right slice.

The AI SDK and related docs in the results emphasize saving and restoring AI state separately from UI state. This supports a strong production pattern: on page load, fetch the durable conversation history from the server, use it to initialize the chat state, and reconstruct local UI state as needed from metadata or defaults. That makes reloads and cross-device continuity much more reliable than a purely in-memory client store.

Server ownership also makes context management easier. Results mention practical patterns like storing metadata such as turn counts and summary checkpoints, then trimming or summarizing older turns as conversations grow. This matters because Claude may require the relevant prior history to be resent, but that does not mean you should resend every raw token forever; a server-side compaction strategy is usually healthier.

Client-held history is easier for users or malicious scripts to tamper with before it reaches your backend. If the conversation affects billing, permissions, tool execution, or regulated content, trusting the browser as the sole history owner is risky. A server-owned or hybrid model lets you validate ownership, tool traces, and message integrity before each Claude request.

There are still valid cases for primarily client-side history. If the app is anonymous, privacy-sensitive, offline-friendly, or intentionally ephemeral, keeping history in the browser and not persisting it remotely may be the right product choice. In those cases, just remember that Claude still needs the relevant history sent on every API request, so “local-first” changes where state lives, not the API’s stateless nature.
A useful rule of thumb is:
Enterprise copilots, agents, tools, regulated apps
: server-owned canonical history is strongly preferred.
: client-only or local-only may be intentional.
For most serious Claude apps, a good architecture is:
manages live UI state in the client.
and often only the latest message, not necessarily the full local transcript.
The server loads the canonical conversation history from storage.
The server appends the new turn, validates the history, and sends the model-relevant subset to Claude.
The server persists the final assistant turn and returns streamed UI updates to the client.
That gives you responsive UX with server-trusted continuity.

A strong concise explanation is: “Claude’s API is stateless, so the application must own conversation history and resend relevant prior turns with each request. For simple apps, client-managed state with

is enough, but for production systems I usually use a hybrid model: the client manages the live rendered transcript and optimistic UI, while the server owns the canonical persisted history, message IDs, tool traces, and context compaction. That gives me reload resilience, cross-device continuity, safer tool workflows, and tighter control over exactly what history gets sent back to Claude.”
